dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to EMT unit for unconscious/fainting/near-fainting at --Address Redacted--, [CITY].
Ma'am, I'm Alex. What brought you to call 911 today?
She was unconscious and seizing when I got back at 5:30. I found her on the floor next to the hospital bed.
When she was conscious, she mentioned head and neck pain only.
She has swelling around her right eye. Pupils are unequal-right non-reactive at 5 mm and left reactive at 4 mm.
Right side of the body is flaccid, with no response to passive movement.
When she is alert, she rates the pain around 6.
Her initial blood pressure is 198 over 98, pulse 116, respiratory rate 24, SpO2 at 97, and glucose is 120. EKG shows sinus tachycardia.
Next reading is 162 over 93, pulse 106, respiratory rate 16, SpO2 97. Glucose is zero. EKG still shows sinus tachycardia.
Now blood pressure is 152 over 89, respiratory rate 14, SpO2 98. EKG is in sinus rhythm with glucose still at zero.
Blood pressure is 135 over 93, pulse 104, respiratory rate 16, SpO2 96. EKG is back to sinus tachycardia.
Her final vitals before arrival at the hospital are blood pressure 126 over 75, SpO2 97, respiratory rate 16, and pulse 105. EKG still in sinus tachycardia.
We found no signs of jugular vein distension.
We started with 4 lead ECG monitoring at 16:51 and later obtained a 12 lead ECG at 16:55.
The 12-lead ECG interpretation during the field assessment showed findings consistent with a left bundle branch block and sinus tachycardia.
We documented respiratory rate at 14 during one of the vital checks.
We also recorded respiratory rate at 16 during another check.
After a couple of attempts, we placed an IV in her left arm with a 20-gauge needle in her extremity vein.
We placed her on 6 liters per minute of oxygen via nasal cannula during transport.
While in between seizures, she was alert to verbal stimuli and could open her eyes independently.
Her pulse was strong and palpable in all extremities, with PMSx4 present in the left side but flaccid in the right.
Her Glasgow Coma Scale was 9 (eye = 2, verbal = 3, motor = 4) on initial arrival.
The patient is secured on the Reeves stretcher with three seat belts and placed in semi-Fowler's position for easier breathing.
It was difficult to get to the patient - I had to climb through a window to reach her.
[COMPANY_NAME] is the closest with a full stroke team and is equipped to handle the intracranial hemorrhagic stroke field impression.
The patient will be moved using a draw sheet pull from the ambulance stretcher to the hospital bed with five seat belts for security.

The patient's current medications include: insulin human, cyclobenzaprine, calcium acetate, pantoprazole, Lasix, cetirizine, gabapentin, carvedilol, duloxetine, amlodipine, trazodone, simvastatin, hydrocodone, calcitriol, lisinopril, and allopurinol. Allergies to Percocet.
Patient's past medical history includes diabetes, CHF, stroke (CVA), and primary hypertension.
During the assessment, we noted consistent eye deviation to the right, which didn't respond to stimuli.
After securing the IV, we administered 300 mL of normal saline.
At 18:00, vitals show 162/93 mmHg, HR 106, respirations 16, SpO2 97, and sinus tachycardia.
By 18:02, vitals were 152/89 mmHg, HR 104, respirations 14, SpO2 98, and sinus rhythm.
Midazolam (Versed) 5 mg was given intramuscularly during the initial seizing episode between 17:53 and 17:55.
At 18:07, vitals had improved slightly to 135/93 mmHg, HR 104, respiratory rate 16, SpO2 96, and sinus tachycardia.
At 18:04, the patient received a second dose of 2.5 mg IV Midazolam for a fifth seizure.
The patient did not require further medication after the IV dose of Midazolam.
Pain level remained consistent at 6 while she was alert.
No new chief complaints reported during reassessment after arriving on scene and throughout transport.
Glucose was continually measured and remained at 0 following initial tests as noted in the ePCR.
We handed over with final vitals at 18:17 to Rokisha Smith, RN.
Ambulance ODEMSA was registered at the hospital's receiving area and unsigned out after standard decontamination.
Granddaughter reported the patient has a prior stroke with left-sided deficits and a current condition affecting the right side.
The patient was transported with full EKG monitoring and IV access in place for optimal care during movement and arrival.
Skin is warm, dry, and intact. No rashes, bruises, or signs of trauma visible.
Head and neck appeared intact with trachea midline and no signs of trauma.
Breathing is regular with clear bilateral breath sounds and no signs of trauma to the chest or back.
Abdomen is soft, non-tender, and non-distended.
PMSx4 present in the left extremities with flaccid right side observed.
All interventions were based upon protocols for altered mental status and seizure management.
We followed the medical protocols from initial assessment to hospital handover.
Final notes, vital signs, and medications were documented and confirmed by the receiving nurse.
Using a draw sheet, the patient was safely transferred to the ER bed with five cross-chest straps.
Pupils remain unchanged with regard to size and reactivity as previously recorded.
Signs of pain were localized to the head and neck.
All secure transport protocols were followed for injury prevention during transfer.
Respiratory rate remained stable at 16, within the normal range during the last assessments.
12-lead tracing was reviewed by hospital staff, confirming sinus rhythm consistent with the field diagnosis.
Final report included a review of cardiac rhythm, status of seizures, and consistent GCS of 9 at destination.
Special transport notes for extrication and movement were handed over with the stretcher handling.
We confirmed transfer treatment by the hospital stroke unit during destination check-in.
All patient care reports, PCR, and interventions are fully documented in the PCR.
Monitoring devices were transitioned for hospital use upon handoff.
The patient was transported in stable condition with comprehensive reports provided upon arrival.
IV was flushed upon completion and proven functional at the hospital intake.
All drug notes and dosing were verified by the receiving RN.
Glasgow Coma Score was consistent at 9 during periods of alertness.
The patient was moved to the ER bed using a draw sheet and cross straps as per transport checklist guidelines.
Medication box was exchanged for future EMS use on completion of this call.
No further action needed by the EMS team after handoff as all interventions were complete and transmitted.
